preFix.title: Soft caramels
preFix.subtitle: Chewy, buttery caramels cut into squares
preFix.excerpt: Soft caramels are made from cream, butter, sugar, and golden syrup cooked together to around 120 degrees C, poured into a tin, and cut into squares when set. The result is a dense, slightly chewy sweet with a deep caramel flavour. They take about 30 minutes of active work and set overnight. Wrapped in baking paper, they make excellent gifts.
preFix.sourceNotes: Soft caramels are a well-established category of British and American confectionery, documented in domestic recipe books from the mid-nineteenth century onward. The basic formula of sugar, cream, butter, and syrup cooked to the firm ball stage appears consistently across British and American sources. The use of glucose or golden syrup to prevent crystallisation and promote chewiness is a consistent feature of professional recipes. Eliza Acton's Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845) includes early caramel preparations.

content.text: The temperature at which you take the caramel off the heat determines the texture. At 115 degrees C it will be very soft and almost pourable when cool. At 122 degrees C it will be firm, chewy, and sliceable. At 127 degrees C and above it becomes hard toffee. The target for soft caramels is 118 to 122 degrees C. A sugar thermometer is not optional for this recipe.

Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper, leaving an overhang. In a medium, heavy saucepan, combine 300 g caster sugar, 75 g golden syrup, 200 ml double cream, and 50 g diced butter. Stir over a medium heat until the sugar dissolves and the butter melts. Increase the heat to medium-high and cook, stirring frequently, until the temperature reaches 118 to 122 degrees C on a sugar thermometer. The mixture will bubble actively, deepen in colour, and smell of caramel. This takes 15 to 20 minutes.

Remove from the heat and stir in 1 tsp vanilla extract. Pour into the prepared tin without scraping the pan sides: the slightly more cooked caramel on the sides may crystallise and cause graininess. Scatter 1 tsp flaked salt evenly over the top. Leave to cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 8 hours. Lift out using the paper overhang and cut into squares with a sharp knife. Wrap each square in a piece of baking paper.

items.problem: The caramels are sticky and do not hold their shape
items.solution: The mixture did not reach 118 degrees C. Even a degree or two below the target makes a significant difference in the final texture. Return the batch to the saucepan and cook to the correct temperature.

items.problem: The caramel seized and turned grainy when the cream was added
items.solution: The cream was too cold, causing the hot sugar to seize. Use cream at room temperature, and add it in a slow, steady stream while stirring rather than pouring it in all at once.
